description.s: March 2026 — A review of the system's philosophical foundations vs. current capabilities.
catalogs the full possibility space. This item asks a different question:
where does the system fall short of its own stated philosophy?
The review measures Hobson against its five inspirations (
): Conway's Humane Dozen, Smalltalk's image persistence, Lisp's REPL-first development, Obenauer's itemized OS, and Obsidian-style linking.
(actions are reversible, system doesn't break), Smalltalk's
For Hobson to be a primary information manager, you need to trust it won't lose your data. The current story is thin:
A browser data clear, a profile migration, or a corrupted IDB wipes everything. The only backup is manual JSON export.
No undo at the system level.
calls out undo/redo for the editor, but the problem is broader: deleting an item, removing an attachment, changing a type — all irreversible.
describes the concept but is marked low priority. For a daily-driver workspace, the inability to see what changed or revert is a serious gap.

Automatic snapshots. Even something simple — a periodic full-dump to a downloadable blob, or a second IDB store holding the last N states of each item. This is the "if I lose my data I stop using the system" problem and should be addressed before anything else gets polished. (Note: the git export workflow to src/items/*.json provides a partial safety net — changes are version-controlled and recoverable. But this doesn't cover accidental IDB loss without a recent export.)
doc already identifies the "seven conceptual hops" problem. But the discoverability gap extends beyond understanding the system to finding your own data:
searches names and filters by type/tag, but there is no way to search inside
across all items. For a growing knowledge base, this is crippling.
describes the idea but hasn't been started.
No snap-to-grid, no alignment guides, no grouping/stacking, no minimap. The spatial metaphor becomes chaotic as workspace size grows.
Full-text search across Active Text bodies is probably the highest-leverage discoverability feature. The
AST is structured — an inverted index could be built as a library item that updates on
events. Buildable as a userland library item — which would itself demonstrate the system's extensibility.
says Hob should be "the obvious choice." But the infrastructure still privileges JS:
— the infrastructure that makes everything work is opaque to Hob. Understanding the system still requires reading JS.
Good progress, but the remaining 26 include critical views (
at 295 lines). The conversion pace vs. the "obvious choice" aspiration creates a long tail.
is silently ignored. Code that looks correct but does nothing is a trust problem for anyone reading the system.
childEnv bug — it undermines the "transparent" philosophy when internal machinery silently ignores its inputs.
correctly identifies this as Priority 1. The specific gaps (undo/redo, copy/paste, multi-block selection, block reordering) are all things users hit within minutes of sustained writing. Emphasis:
Every other editing gap has workarounds (awkward but survivable). No undo means fear of editing. This directly undermines "Robust" and "Continuous."
matters more than internal copy/paste. If you can't paste text from a web page or another app, Hobson can't be a primary note-taking tool.
(Alt+Up/Down) would be more natural than drag-and-drop for a keyboard-centric system.
not philosophy violations. The system handles its chosen domain (text, code, structured data) well. These are feature gaps that limit daily use as a PIM:

A personal information manager that can't store screenshots, diagrams, or file attachments is fundamentally limited. IndexedDB can store blobs. Even just pasting images into notes (stored as base64 or blob references) would be transformative.
Markdown files, CSV, bookmarks, OPML — bringing data into Hobson requires writing import scripts. The
library exists, but there's no UI for file import.

is "not started." For task management and personal organization, the inability to associate dates with items (due dates, reminders, scheduled reviews) is a gap. The task tag exists but there's no date field convention.
Image support in Active Text (paste to blob,
AST node) and a drag-and-drop file import UI would open the door to Hobson as a real daily-use PIM.
These aren't philosophy violations yet, but are load-bearing assumptions that could fail at scale:
333 items currently. What happens at 3,000? The
API presumably does full scans of IndexedDB. The spatial canvas renders all attached items simultaneously. The Active Text editor walks entire ASTs. None of these have documented scaling strategies.
Module cache invalidation is actively painful.
This is not hypothetical — it causes daily friction.

documents the root cause and the "Bob pattern" fix: saving any code item triggers clearCache(), and any module with state in module-scoped variables (viewport-manager, selection-manager, related-items-lib) loses that state. Keyboard shortcuts silently break, the related-items widget goes stale, selection state is lost. The globalThis workaround and viewport:ready event migration are partial fixes, but several items still need migration (test-runner-service, test-run-result-list-view, error-list handler). The deeper question — targeted cache invalidation vs. full clear — remains open. This directly undermines the "edit code, see changes immediately" workflow that is the Smalltalk soul of the system.

Conway's "Always On" attribute is only partially achieved — the system requires an open browser tab. A service worker could enable offline caching of the bootloader, installability as a desktop app, and (eventually) background processing for reminders or sync.
Active Text parity is correctly Priority 1 (it's the daily-use blocker)
Doc query helpers (Priority 2) address real verbosity in eval blocks
Hob view conversions (Priority 3) move toward conceptual uniformity
Hob correctness & stdlib (Priority 4) fixes real bugs
The gaps identified here are largely orthogonal — they represent things the roadmap doesn't cover, not disagreements with what it does cover.
Ranked by impact on the "can I use Hobson as my primary workspace" question:
— the daily-use blocker. Without it, sustained writing involves constant low-level anxiety. Agrees with the roadmap's Priority 1.

— finish the viewport:ready migration for remaining items, and investigate targeted cache invalidation vs. full clear. The "edit code, see changes immediately" workflow is the Smalltalk soul of the system; every silent breakage after a code save undermines it.
— buildable as a userland library + index over Active Text ASTs. As item count grows past ~333, name-based search becomes inadequate. Would also be a showcase for the system's extensibility.

— periodic auto-export or IDB versioning. The git export workflow provides a partial safety net today, but an in-system auto-snapshot would close the remaining gap and become critical if Hobson is ever shared with other users.
Full-text search (#3) can be built entirely within the system as a library item, which would itself demonstrate Hobson's extensibility.
